rituals]] [[Category:伝統芸能|en]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:q--1]] [[ja:御田植祭]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}} * {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}} * {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}} * {{ill|Ota-mai|ja|御田舞}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== Further Reading == * 『年中行事大辞典』 (Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Annual Events) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009). ISBN 978-4-642-01443-4. * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』 (Toyama’s 100 Cultural Treasures, Series 3: Festivals) (Toyama Prefectural Board of Education, 2007).
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf Toyama Cultural Heritage 100 (Festivals Section) - Toyama Prefecture Board of Education]

Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. == Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

rituals]] [[Category:伝統芸能|en]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:q--1]] [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:御田植祭]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

rituals]] [[Category:伝統芸能|en]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:q--1]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:御田植祭]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

rituals]] [[Category:伝統芸能|en]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:q--1]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:御田植祭]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

== Further Reading == * 『年中行事大辞典』 (Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Annual Events) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009). ISBN 978-4-642-01443-4. * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』 (Toyama’s 100 Cultural Treasures, Series 3: Festivals) (Toyama Prefectural Board of Education, 2007).

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}} * {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}} * {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}} * {{ill|Ota-mai|ja|御田舞}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== Further Reading == * 『年中行事大辞典』 (Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Annual Events) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009). ISBN 978-4-642-01443-4. * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』 (Toyama’s 100 Cultural Treasures, Series 3: Festivals) (Toyama Prefectural Board of Education, 2007).
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf Toyama Cultural Heritage 100 (Festivals Section) - Toyama Prefecture Board of Education]

While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.

=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

rituals]] [[Category:伝統芸能|en]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:q--1]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[ja:御田植祭]] [[Category:New]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

rituals]] [[Category:伝統芸能|en]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:q--1]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[ja:御田植祭]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Otaue Festival]][[Category:qq]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

== Further Reading == * 『年中行事大辞典』 (Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Annual Events) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009). ISBN 978-4-642-01443-4. * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』 (Toyama’s 100 Cultural Treasures, Series 3: Festivals) (Toyama Prefectural Board of Education, 2007).

[[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[ja:御田植祭]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Otaue Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Otaue Festival]][[Category:qq]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}} * {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}} * {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}} * {{ill|Ota-mai|ja|御田舞}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== Further Reading == * 『年中行事大辞典』 (Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Annual Events) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009). ISBN 978-4-642-01443-4. * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』 (Toyama’s 100 Cultural Treasures, Series 3: Festivals) (Toyama Prefectural Board of Education, 2007).

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:bumping to try to fix the sexagenary stuff]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:bumping to try to fix the sexagenary stuff]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}} * {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}} * {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}} * {{ill|Ota-mai|ja|御田舞}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== Further Reading == * 『年中行事大辞典』 (Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Annual Events) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009). ISBN 978-4-642-01443-4. * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』 (Toyama’s 100 Cultural Treasures, Series 3: Festivals) (Toyama Prefectural Board of Education, 2007).
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf Toyama Cultural Heritage 100 (Festivals Section) - Toyama Prefecture Board of Education]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.

Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Otaue Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Otaue Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Otaue Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Otaue Festival]][[Category:qq]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).

== Further Reading == * 『年中行事大辞典』 (Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Annual Events) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009). ISBN 978-4-642-01443-4. * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』 (Toyama’s 100 Cultural Treasures, Series 3: Festivals) (Toyama Prefectural Board of Education, 2007).

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Completion tier 2]] to [[Category:Completion tier 3]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 17, 2023}} [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|thumb|right|240px|{{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}}]] '''Otaue Matsuri''' (御田植祭) refers to ceremonies conducted at {{ill|temples and shrines|ja|寺社}} or on {{ill|imperial estates|ja|領田}} to celebrate rice planting.
== Overview == This is a {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事}} that involves reenacting the steps of {{ill|Agriculture|lt=agricultural work|ja|農作業}} to pray for a {{ill|Bumper crop|lt=bountiful harvest|ja|豊作}}. Ceremonies held during the actual rice planting season are called "Otaue Matsuri," while those held during {{ill|Japanese New Year|lt=New Year|ja|正月}} as celebratory pre-harvest performances are generally known as "Tan-asobi." However, the term "Otaue Matsuri" often encompasses both.
These events are widespread across Japan, reflecting the Japanese people's long and deep connection with rice cultivation. While the organizations conducting these ceremonies vary by region, few rituals include all stages of agricultural production. Most focus solely on rice planting and are termed "Otaue Matsuri."
== Origins == The origin of Otaue Matsuri is believed to lie in the custom of singing {{ill|rice-planting songs|ja|田植歌}} to make the physically demanding work of planting rice more enjoyable. Over time, this practice merged with agrarian rituals to pray to {{ill|ta no kami|ja|田の神}} (field deities) for fertility, developing into a formal festival. Historically, the encouragement of agriculture by powerful landowners led to the spread and adaptation of these ceremonies across various regions. Consequently, these rituals are often transmitted as {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} at prominent regional temples and shrines. While reenacting agricultural work remains central, unique regional cultural elements, including traditional performing arts, have been incorporated.
== Prominent Otaue Matsuri == === Japan’s Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals === * {{ill|Katori Jingū Otaue Festival|ja|香取神宮御田植祭}} in {{ill|Katori|ja|香取市}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}.<ref name="katori">[https://www.city.katori.lg.jp/sightseeing/gyoji/haru/s_otaue.html Katori Jingū Otaue Matsuri] - Katori City</ref> * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮}} in {{ill|Shima|ja|志摩市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, also known as the {{ill|Isobe-no-Ogōn-den|ja|磯部の御神田}} (designated as an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="shima">[https://www.kanko-shima.com/html/event_det.php?no=20150505021458 Izawa-no-Miya Otaue Festival: One of Japan's Three Great Rice-Planting Festivals] - Shima Tourism Association</ref> * {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}}’s {{ill|Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival|ja|住吉の御田植}}, in {{ill|Sumiyoshi Ward|ja|住吉区}}, {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪府}} (also an Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property).<ref name="katori" /><ref name="shima" />
=== Other Notable Otaue Festivals === * {{ill|Tsutsukowake Shrine|ja|都々古別神社}}’s Otaue Festival in {{ill|Tanakura|ja|棚倉町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * The Otaue Festival of {{ill|Keitoku Inari Shrine|ja|慶徳稲荷神社}} in {{ill|Kitakata|ja|喜多方市}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Isasumi Shrine|ja|伊佐須美神社}} in {{ill|Aizu Misato|ja|会津美里町}}, Fukushima Prefecture (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property). * {{ill|Chichibu Shrine|ja|秩父神社}}’s {{ill|Chichibu Shrine Otaue Matsuri|ja|秩父神社御田植祭}} in {{ill|Chichibu|ja|秩父市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} (designated as a Saitama Prefectural Cultural Property). * {{ill|Shimo-mura Kamo Shrine|ja|下村加茂神社}} in {{ill|Imizu|ja|射水市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}} (designated as a Toyama Prefectural Cultural Property and selected for the Toyama Cultural Heritage 100). * {{ill|Hirohata Shrine|ja|広峯神社}} in {{ill|Himeji|ja|姫路市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}} (designated as a Himeji City Intangible Cultural Property). * {{ill|Kibitsu-hiko Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}} in {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. * {{ill|Fuse Shrine|ja|布勢神社}} in {{ill|Ōkita|ja|東区}}, Okayama Prefecture (designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Property).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://afw-at.com/page_e.php?id=432|title=Fuse Shrine Otaue Festival|publisher=At Town Web Magazine|accessdate=2019-03-28}}</ref> * {{ill|Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Kitsuki)|ja|若宮八幡社}} in {{ill|Kitsuki|ja|杵築市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}} (designated as an Ōita Prefectural Cultural Property).
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}} * {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}} * {{ill|Taue Odori|ja|田植踊}} * {{ill|Ota-mai|ja|御田舞}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== Further Reading == * 『年中行事大辞典』 (Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Annual Events) (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2009). ISBN 978-4-642-01443-4. * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』 (Toyama’s 100 Cultural Treasures, Series 3: Festivals) (Toyama Prefectural Board of Education, 2007).
== External Links == * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf Toyama Cultural Heritage 100 (Festivals Section) - Toyama Prefecture Board of Education]
